name: Kumina
description: Kumina is an Afro-Jamaican religious tradition that combines ancestral veneration, spirit possession, and traditional healing practices. Originating from the Congo region of Africa, it was brought to Jamaica by enslaved Africans. Kumina focuses on communicating with ancestral spirits (zombies) and deities through drumming, singing, dancing, and ritual sacrifice. The primary goal is to maintain harmony between the living and the dead, ensuring the well-being of the community. Rituals often involve invoking specific spirits for guidance, protection, or healing. The rhythmic drumming is central, believed to induce trance states and facilitate spirit possession. Kumina is deeply intertwined with Jamaican cultural identity and represents a powerful expression of African heritage and resistance against oppression. It emphasizes the importance of community, tradition, and the interconnectedness of the spiritual and physical realms. The practice also incorporates knowledge of herbs and traditional medicine for healing purposes. While often practiced in secret, Kumina remains a vital part of Jamaica's cultural landscape, offering a pathway to spiritual power and ancestral connection.
subtitle: Ancestral veneration and spirit possession.
associatedDeities: African deities adapted to Jamaican context

keyMyths.title: Origin of Kumina Drums
keyMyths.description: The drums are said to be a gift from the ancestors, carrying the power to connect with the spirit world. The specific rhythms unlock different levels of consciousness.

keyMyths.title: The First Kumina Queen
keyMyths.description: Legend speaks of a woman who led her people in resistance using Kumina rituals, empowering them with spiritual strength. Her spirit is invoked for guidance and protection.

sources.author: Joseph Moore
sources.work: Religion of Jamaican Negroes: A Study of Afro-Jamaican Acculturation
sources.description: An early academic study of Kumina and other Afro-Jamaican religions.
